ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has an entry for Chinese broccoli as a raw ingredient (FOODON_03301710). No exact entry exists for the stir-fried preparation with garlic; closest_parent used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Chinese kale (Brassica oleracea var. alboglabra) — cooked vegetable dish
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for stir-fried kailan with garlic. A00LC (Chinese kale) used as closest parent.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code found for this specific stir-fried vegetable dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of stir-fried kailan with minced garlic is approximately 200 g, served as a shared side dish alongside rice and other main courses.

nutrient_source.title: Kailan, cooked — HPB Singapore Foodo / USDA FoodData Central Chinese broccoli cooked (NDB 11977) adapted for stir-fried preparation with oil and oyster sauce
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Foodo lists cooked kailan (Chinese broccoli) nutrient values. Values adapted to reflect stir-frying with approximately 5 g vegetable oil per 100 g and a small amount of oyster sauce, consistent with standard Singapore hawker/restaurant preparation. USDA FoodData Central NDB 11977 (Chinese broccoli, cooked, boiled, drained, without salt) used as secondary cross-reference for baseline vegetable macros. Energy and fat adjusted upward to account for cooking oil; sodium adjusted upward for oyster sauce seasoning.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Non-Starchy Vegetables — analogous estimate based on cooked leafy green vegetables (GI ≈ 10–20)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for stir-fried kailan with garlic. The University of Sydney GI Database consistently reports GI values of 10–20 for cooked non-starchy leafy green vegetables. A GI of 15 is assigned as a conservative midpoint estimate for this dish, consistent with the low-carbohydrate, high-fibre profile of cooked kailan. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (15 × 10.4) / 100 = 1.56, rounded to 1.6.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from oyster sauce seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Each 200 g serving provides approximately 640 mg sodium, which represents about 28% of the 2300 mg daily limit recommended for hypertensive individuals; patients should request reduced-sauce preparation or limit portion size.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index and low net carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 15 and only 5.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g, stir-fried kailan with garlic has a negligible glycemic load and is a suitable vegetable side dish for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Favourable fat profile and high micronutrient density
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The dish provides predominantly unsaturated fats from vegetable oil, negligible cholesterol, and cardioprotective micronutrients including vitamin C, folate, and potassium, making it a heart-healthy vegetable choice when sodium intake is monitored.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Stir-fried kailan with minced garlic (蒜蓉小芥蓝) is a classic Chinese vegetable side dish widely served in Singapore's Chinese restaurants, zi char stalls, and seafood restaurants. Kailan, also known as Chinese broccoli or Chinese kale (Brassica oleracea var. alboglabra), is a dark leafy green vegetable with thick stems and broad leaves that has a mildly bitter, slightly peppery flavour. In this preparation, the kailan is blanched briefly or stir-fried directly in a hot wok with a generous amount of minced garlic, vegetable oil, and oyster sauce, resulting in tender-crisp stems and wilted leaves coated in a savoury, aromatic garlic sauce. The dish is characterised by its vibrant green colour, garlicky aroma, and balanced bitter-savoury taste. Nutritionally, stir-fried kailan with garlic is low in calories (approximately 72 kcal per 100 g), low in carbohydrates (5.2 g per 100 g), and provides meaningful amounts of dietary fibre (2.4 g), vitamin C (28 mg), folate (45 mcg DFE), calcium (72 mg), and iron (1.0 mg). The fat content is moderate (4.5 g per 100 g) due to cooking oil, with a favourable unsaturated fat profile. The primary clinical consideration is sodium content from oyster sauce (approximately 320 mg per 100 g), which is relevant for individuals managing hypertension. For individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, the dish is highly suitable due to its very low glycemic index (estimated GI ≈ 15) and low net carbohydrate content. The dish is commonly ordered as a shared side dish alongside steamed rice, roasted meats, or seafood dishes in a typical Chinese family-style meal setting in Singapore.
common_user_queries: How many calories in stir-fried kailan with garlic?
Is Chinese broccoli the same as kailan?
Can hypertensive patients eat stir-fried kailan?
meal_context: Shared side dish with steamed rice
Healthy vegetable option in Chinese cuisine

confidence_reason: No exact HPB Foodo entry exists for stir-fried kailan with minced garlic as a prepared dish. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Foodo cooked kailan baseline and USDA FoodData Central Chinese broccoli cooked (NDB 11977), with adjustments for stir-frying oil and oyster sauce seasoning consistent with standard Singapore restaurant preparation. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database data for cooked non-starchy leafy greens. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted estimates based on HPB Foodo cooked kailan and USDA NDB 11977, adjusted for approximately 5 g vegetable oil per 100 g and oyster sauce seasoning. Exact values will vary by restaurant preparation. 2. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients contributing B12; oyster sauce contributes negligible B12). 3. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — no vitamin D-contributing ingredients in this dish). 4. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero — no hydrogenated fats used in standard preparation). 5. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — no animal fat or cholesterol-containing ingredients in standard preparation; oyster sauce contributes negligible cholesterol). 6. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA NDB 11977 and HPB Foodo cooked kailan data; these are not -1 sentinel values but estimated numeric values. 7. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this specific dish was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. 8. The dish is not strictly vegan due to oyster sauce (mollusc-derived); is_vegetarian is set to false for the same reason. 9. Halal status is marked true as standard preparation uses vegetable oil and oyster sauce without pork-derived ingredients, consistent with common Singapore halal-certified restaurant practice, though individual restaurant certification should be verified.
